speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number four in the interview with Maria Glickman. Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to about where I stopped.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the truth.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had taken 50 girls--

speaker: interviewee
text: To Strassenhof. And when we came there, they said to us that we will not go out. We just work inside the concentration camp. Because before us, 15 girls runned. They catch them. Three girls run, they didn't. They did go to the partisan. But 15, they were catched.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we worked inside the concentration camp. And I got to know my husband. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he liked me. I-- He was--

speaker: interviewee
text: --I didn't have my hair on my head. I forgot to tell this.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a prisoner?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. They cut my hair in Kaiserwald. I forgot to tell it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You could tell it now.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working in [NON-ENGLISH]. And 12 people ran away. And I was between the 12 who could run. But I didn't, because of my girlfriend. We got like sisters. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they called the German SS. And they came. And they looked for the people who run away, but they didn't find them. One lady had her husband, not Jewish. And he came with a two horses and a van. And four of the people got inside, some run alone. I remember a beautiful musician in '39. He got a gold saxophone from the government. He run away where he was living before. And the lady hold there 16 people up to the moment that the German came-- the Russian came. And I stayed. I didn't run. They want to take me. And I said, no, I'm not going.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we came back in concentration camp, I was a [NON-ENGLISH]. That means I was responsible for 10 people. We had 178 people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I helped out, because I-- I spoke German. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the main boss, [INAUDIBLE] was the main boss. [INAUDIBLE] is like a-- a general-- a general major. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he called me to come. And he asked me questions. He put a piece of [NON-ENGLISH] from the machine, a piece of [INAUDIBLE]. You know? And he's gone on the table. And he hold me an hour and a half. How did they run away? How come I didn't know? Why didn't I tell? I said, I didn't know nothing. The people that ran were amongst the 10 that you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Among the 12.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that you were responsible for?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, among the 12 who run together and four run separate. They run-- I didn't know where they run. I just know that my [INAUDIBLE], the lady who had a husband, he came with his cousin. And he saw me through the through the-- toilet, excuse me. And he said to her, take your friend. I will hold her. And I said, no, I didn't go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, then after we came home-- home, in concentration camp, [INAUDIBLE] asked me to come. And he asked me questions. And I told him that I really didn't know where they did go. But I would never tell, that's for sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said to me-- by the end, he said to me, [NON-ENGLISH]. That means my hair-- my hair must be cut. And I must have the striped dresses. So I said, thank you, Herr Kommandant. Danke, Herr Kommandant. That means I thank him.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, just a moment, why? I said, my head is here. Hair will grow. He said, out of here. So, I did go out. Twice he helped me. That's true. Twice he helped.

speaker: interviewee
text: Once he helped me when we came. I don't know if I told you. We came from work. And everybody who came had to get undressed. Me, too, everybody. And there was a lady before me. And she had three pair of pants. She took the pants, must be to sell to buy a piece of bread, I don't know what. And she had bread. And she said to me, take my bread. I said, no. I was scared. So, the SS lady, the Blitzfrau [INAUDIBLE], gave me one. So I took my other arm. And I want to take the bread. So she gave me another one while I want to take the bread. So, I didn't know what to do. Then she took-- she made a fist. And she beat the lady over the head. And then eggs [INAUDIBLE] [LAUGHS]. And I began to cry and laugh. [LAUGHS] She had three eggs in her hair. She didn't know. I didn't-- nobody knew. And she gave it over the head. And it run, the eggs. And I couldn't stand. So she give me one with her foot.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, Sauer, the general, came in and said, please stop and send her to the barrack. And she send me. Because really it wasn't my fault.

speaker: interviewee
text: But, otherwise, they get undressed. And they get beaten. And if they had what they-- some had could be gold, I don't know-- they took away everything. And then we go to the-- our places. This was one of the nice things that the-- it was terrible. Because in a big place and there were men and women. For a man to get undressed is much worse than for a woman. The men cried like babies. They couldn't-- your know, they hold their hands like that. I will never forget. The girls, not so. But all of us get undressed. And they looked at what we have. Could be some have gold, I don't know. I didn't. This was one fall when we came from work. [LAUGHS] It was terrible times, and terrible things. And you cannot remember in one time everything. But it itches your heart all the time. And you think how people got killed, how your family got killed, how your father, how your brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some had wives they are alive. And their children got killed. And their wives got killed. What can you do? Nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we-- we were there up to October-- no, September, 26 in '43. And my husband told me that in case the-- the Russian will come, the German will kill you-- will kill us for sure. Because they don't have time to take us to another concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is '43 or '44?

speaker: interviewee
text: '4-- '44, September 26 or 25. And he said, I make-- where he worked, he heat the water for the factory, 50,000 liters one place and 30,000 in the other. And under this was a chimney 45 meters tall. And in the chimney, through the place, he would go through. And first of all, he closed up the-- so nobody will know that there is something inside. He closed it with-- with-- with cement, with everything. I don't know. I haven't seen. And then he maked a hole downstairs. And where he find this, I don't know, something to close up. And he find a piece of wood. And he would put the wood to this piece and to the other wall. It was very small. We were supposed to be there 24 people, and we were just 11. The other ones got scared to go in.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, he built a hiding place.

speaker: interviewee
text: A hiding place, 11 months. He brought water in. He stealed a radio. He stealed 350 loaves of bread by the [NON-ENGLISH]. That means the [NON-ENGLISH], a-- a place where all the food comes together, and then they give to the soldiers. So, my husband, in the middle of the night, would go and steal, and through the chimney go down and bring the food. But when we came, the bread was worth nothing. It was green. What we had is, we had for meat, like-- like soup, like goulash, I don't know what, in boxes, and marmalade, and the water and, for three days, two little pieces of bread. So, I gave my bread to my husband. Because he is a man. I didn't need the bread. And what I heard is that one of my friends who came out with me got very sick. She would die if not me. She had-- I didn't think it's cancer. But I didn't know. I was too young. Here under the right arm, so big. She had fever, I think, the highest that it can be. And I said to my girlfriend, the other one, I said, Jeanette, I think I have to open it. Because, otherwise, she will for sure die. I had a needle. So, first I put it in the ground. And then I washed it. And with a clean paper-- not paper-- handkerchief, I cleaned it. And then I-- I asked her friend to hold her arms like this. And I cut, and cut, and cut. And then I open it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And everything did go on me. But five minutes later, she opened her eyes. And I washed it, and cleaned it all night. And in the morning she said, what did you do to me? I said, nothing, I just opened your wound, that's all. She was in the chimney with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she was in the chimney. We were three girls. Her name is [? Sarah. ?] And the other one was [? Jeanette. ?] And I was Maria.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my husband, and seven more.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hanon. In Hebrew, Hanon. They called him Honay. We had a rabbi with us. He was 11 weeks before us. When they came to take from concentration camp the people to Germany, people got scared. They did think they take them to kill them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what they did is, they run. Him and the other man, was-- his name was [INAUDIBLE], they both came to the chimney. They did know-- I don't know how-- where. And they were there 11 weeks before us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I saw them, I didn't want to go back. Because both have eyes like crazy people. Six weeks, no air, but they had food. The food was fresh then. And then we came. And my girlfriend [? Sarah, ?] her boyfriend, tooked her in a bag. And he sit on her. Not on her, he sit by her. And boss of our concentration camp did think that he's sitting like this, that she was there. And at night when everybody was asleep, waiting for them to go to the concentration camp, he walked in quietly through the place in the chimney. Then my husband did go out. He put on pieces of junk, I don't know, something to wear on both hands. He would go to the end and close it, so when the dogs come they wouldn't smell the-- the-- the feet, that somebody walked there. And they closed up. And we heard the German coming in knocking and looking for us. And they make fire under the water. Nothing, we didn't feel. It was hot. And there was holes to the chimney. I would open it. And I would catch in. But we-- we had to do something. We did go out three-- three times, for 18 days and nights, three times.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that it was time to go into hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they took the-- the people to the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, the entire care was emptied out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Liquidated, liquidated. So, when they took the people-- so, we didn't know. So, me and my girlfriend, the SS was walking inside. She opened the window. She got in, hold open the window. And I got in. Well, I closed just the window, put down my head, an SS man walked through. And my other girl, the third girlfriend who I helped, her friend took her in in the baggage.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were in there for 18 days.

speaker: interviewee
text: 18 days and nights. Then, three days before the Russian came, they cut the water off. Not for us, they cut the water off.

speaker: interviewee
text: How did we know that the Russian came? We didn't, because the fire was done. And the water was done.

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband said, from where it goes the steam must go water. It was so big, like a finger. And three men-- my husband, one I don't know if he's alive, one is in Israel, Leva Mil'shtein, they did go out to take water. And when my husband got three boxes of water, a Russian came in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, [NON-ENGLISH]. And my husband stood and looked at him and didn't know what he's saying. So, the other one spoke Russian a little bit. He said, take your hand up. He said, what is he, German? He said, no, he's Jewish. Here is a concentration camp. And there is around and around mines.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, no more mines. 4:00 at night, we take out all the mines. They were since 4:00 there. And we didn't know. Then they said, out all of you. And so, I tried to go out, and I couldn't. I got paralyzed lying on the floor, so they took me out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the-- how was his name? A general from the second army, he said, who speaks good Russian? I said, I speak perfectly Russian. He said, I want to ask you questions and you please answer.

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, of course, I will. So he asked me everything that I know, and I told him. And then they took me. And they took me to wash myself, because I was black. And my hair was so big. And the eyes was scared, so I looked like crazy, must be. And then they took us to the city. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You understood by now that the war was over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and that they had liberated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They said, yes. We saw the Germans running and the Russian coming. We know that it's over, the war. But it wasn't over yet. Just in Riga, it was over. In-- in [INAUDIBLE], where we say, a city in Latvia, there wasn't-- the Germans stood there and shot. Took a couple of weeks to take them in. It wasn't so easy. But we were free.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, I sit on a bench. I couldn't walk. And a lady came, a Russian lady. And she said, young man, how old are you? I say, I'm not a young man, I'm a young lady. And I'm old 19 years. She says, why are you here? I says, by Hitler.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're from concentration camp. I says, yes. Ah, now I know you wear pants, and a jacket, and boots. And you don't have hair. I think you were a-- [LAUGHS] you were a boy. I wasn't a boy. This was the first thing. Then I had very hard by the Russian, because I write to Wilno to send me papers. I didn't get an answer. And the Russian didn't believe me nothing. They don't believe their own brothers. What they did is they gave me a paper. And I had to come to the police every month and sign my name. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In '46 I got a paper to go to Wilno. I came. And the government in Lithuania didn't help me with nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you went back home.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back home. And I didn't get nothing. And I did go back to Riga. But I got-- it was very hard for me. I lived 17 kilometers, like 20 some miles, away from the city. I lived in Kaiserwald in a villa. I lived very-- in a very rich villa, but I didn't have what to eat. They don't-- if you don't work, you don't get food. So my husband get 350 gram bread, but not me. So I had a watch I sold. I had earrings I sold. And I didn't have more, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you still had this jewelery from--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the jewelry they took away.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the watch and the earrings--

speaker: interviewee
text: The watch and the earrings my husband gave me. He had it somewhere in concentration camp. He had where to put it. Where I was, in Kaiserwald, you couldn't put nothing away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you sold that for food?

speaker: interviewee
text: For food, yes, in-- in market by the-- by the Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went back to Wilno, did you go back to where your home had been?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you see there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, just a naked place. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your home gone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. Everything was gone. Not mine-- most of the people. [INAUDIBLE] All they left, a house half-- house and half nothing, or nothing at all. I did go where my mother's sister was living-- nothing. Most of the-- because they didn't want people to talk what they did. This was the Germans. And then it was the Russians with the Lithuanians. Everybody did something to us and didn't want people do talk, so they close it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, when the war was over, nobody was interested in hearing about what had happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. You see, what I know is we would talk between themselves. But nobody-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I tell you, the-- the how do you call it, the place where in Ponary, my friend made that was written about that here was killed 360,000 people in Hebrew and in Jewish. And they take it off. And they [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody from the government [INAUDIBLE] the-- I don't know. Police, somebody-- you can't--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was after the war they changed the monument?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. They changed what was written, that here are lying people killed by Hitler. That's all, nothing else. And then there was two cemeteries.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was one cemetery-- a holy man. He was Christian. And he changed for Jewish. [INAUDIBLE], his son, and they took his-- they burned him alive in-- in the city, the Polish people. So they took the-- when a person burned, what comes out? The ashes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ashes, like in Jewish. And they put it in a grave. And it grows-- did grow a tree like a person-- hand, feet, really. And the Germans didn't touch it. I don't know why. They left it be. But the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know, the Lithuanian or the Russian, they closed both cemeteries. No cemeteries anymore, houses-- new houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where the cemeteries used to be.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They did it in Kiev, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you settle?

speaker: interviewee
text: I settled where my husband was. I didn't want to go to Poland. But he got to know that his mother and two sisters are alive. There were in Siberia.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, what will we go? You don't have nobody. I don't know the language. Let's sit here. So, we sit. 10 years of the 12 and a half, I was most of the time hungry. Yeah, because he worked very hard. But you couldn't make a living.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because if you get 350 gram bread, then you need 600. Because you don't have fat. You don't have eggs. You don't have nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in Riga?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Riga. So we were hungry. I got food for the children from the government, that's true. But I have to walk or to go by train, street train, 17 kilometers a day in one side.

speaker: interviewer
text: When where your children born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My first daughter was born October 11, '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Malka, ?] [? Musia, ?] [? Malka. ?] Her name-- her real name [? is Musia. ?] But she calls herself Molly. It's all right. And my second was born '48, March 10.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sima Glickman. She isn't married. And my husband was born June 23-- 13, in year '23. And mine you have, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you stayed in Riga for 12 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: 12 and a half years. I write to Molotov. You know Molotov?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who was then like here is the Secretary of State. He was a very intelligent man. Nothing to say about him. But he didn't-- to-- to say something just had to say Stalin, nobody else. What he said goed. If you said, this-- you have a suit. And this suit is a good suit, somebody will say. He would say, it's from London. I bought it in '39, '38. You got three years for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you have stealed bread and they catch you with a loaf of bread, they did so-- how many years you worked? How many days you worked? 40 the years? 25 years to Siberia. If you worked good there, you sit 12 years. If not, you die there.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was terrible cold there. It's unbelievable. It's the best air in the world there. But with no food, there is not enough. So people died.

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband, thank to God, didn't sit. And me neither. But what I did is I got to know people from the Jewish theater. And we had to play Sholem Aleichem's You know it? Please explain.

speaker: interviewee
text: The [NON-ENGLISH]. So nine days before we had to play, Stalin said, no Jewish theater, nowhere. No concerts, no dance, no music, nothing in Jewish. So this was the end of my playing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So and other things I couldn't do it. Because I have small children. You can't leave them alone. I didn't have money to pay for somebody to look at them. So we lived up to '57. I gave my papers in '56, in December month. And in '57 marked the first I got the papers. I got alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband didn't go. He was scared. He just came when he had to sign. So when we get to Poland, there I got my real papers. And we were in Poland. The Polish people gave us everything. They gave us where to live, two and a half months, food, train tickets, money, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they knew you were Holocaust survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: They picked us up like Holocaust survivors. Of course, they knew. We came to our country. I was the who was born in Poland. And then when my husband looked for work, he couldn't speak of word of Polish. He couldn't speak good Russian, either. But they liked him. And they gave him a green number to have a taxi, a cab.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we-- we didn't have the money. So a friend of ours had the money. So my husband took his cab. He got 70% and my husband 30%. What would you do? We're going to break here and go to the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK
